--- title: "Part 26: ML Pipelines, Cross-Validation, and Hyperparameter Search" --- [![Open In Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/sambaiga/ds-mlops-path/blob/main/tutorials/04-ml-intro/05-sklearn-pipeline.ipynb) [![View Source on GitHub](https://img.shields.io/badge/Source-GitHub-181717.svg?logo=github)](https://github.com/sambaiga/ds-mlops-path/blob/main/tutorials/04-ml-intro/05-sklearn-pipeline.ipynb) **DS-MLOps Part 4: ML Landscape** **Python 3.12+ | Author: Anthony Faustine** ## Before you begin Part 25 built models on six numerical features and showed how a single split can give a misleading score. This notebook adds two essential production-grade idioms: 1. **`Pipeline`**: chain preprocessing and modelling into one object so there is no way to accidentally leak test data into training. 2. **`ColumnTransformer`**: route different feature types (numerical, categorical) through different transformers in one declarative step. You will also go beyond the default hyperparameters with three search strategies -- `GridSearchCV`, `RandomizedSearchCV`, and Optuna -- and finish by interpreting the model's feature weights and writing a brief model card. > Callout markers used throughout this notebook are explained on the [book cover page](../../index.qmd#callout-guide). ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" icon=false} ## Topics covered | Topic | Why it matters | |---|---| | **Pipeline** | Prevents data leakage inside cross-validation automatically | | **ColumnTransformer** | Apply different transformers to numerical and categorical features in one step | | **Leakage demonstration** | See the numeric difference in score between a leaky and a correct approach | | **GridSearchCV** | Exhaustive search for best hyperparameters; correct when the space is small | | **RandomizedSearchCV** | Sample from distributions; better for larger search spaces | | **Optuna** | Bayesian-style search that learns from past trials; production-grade tool | | **Feature importance** | Inspect which features drive predictions; validate model sanity | | **Model card** | Structured documentation for every trained model | ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" icon=false} ## Learning Objectives By the end of Part 26 you will be able to: | # | Skill | Covered in | |---|---|---| | 1 | Build a Pipeline that chains preprocessing and a model into one object | Sec. 1 | | 2 | Use ColumnTransformer to apply different transformers to different feature types | Sec. 2 | | 3 | Explain why a Pipeline prevents data leakage inside cross-validation | Sec. 3 | | 4 | Search hyperparameters with GridSearchCV and RandomizedSearchCV | Sec. 4 | | 5 | Run an Optuna study to optimise pipeline hyperparameters efficiently | Sec. 5 | | 6 | Extract feature importance from a fitted pipeline and interpret it | Sec. 6 | | 7 | Document a trained model with a structured model card | Sec. 7 | ::: ## 0. Setup ::: {#1e7964dd .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} from __future__ import annotations from pathlib import Path from typing import Any import warnings import joblib import numpy as np import optuna import pandas as pd from great_tables import GT, md as gt_md from loguru import logger from lets_plot import * from scipy.stats import loguniform from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer from sklearn.dummy import DummyClassifier from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression from sklearn.metrics import ( classification_report, f1_score, ) from sklearn.model_selection import ( GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV, StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score, train_test_split, ) from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, StandardScaler from ark.plot.theme import modern_theme from ark.plot.tokens import BRAND_PALETTE, INFO, DANGER, SUCCESS, WARNING, AI_ACCENT from ark.plot.gt_style import themed_gt, metrics_report optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING) LetsPlot.setup_html() warnings.filterwarnings("ignore") logger.info("Setup complete") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: We load the same hotel booking dataset from Part 25, but this time we keep both numerical and categorical features. The categorical columns describe the booking channel, hotel type, meal plan, and customer profile -- all useful signals for predicting cancellation, but they require encoding before any sklearn model can use them. ::: {#100c40a6 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} DATA_URL = ( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/" "master/data/2020/2020-02-11/hotels.csv" ) DATA_PATH = Path("data/hotel_bookings.csv") def load_hotel_data(path: Path = DATA_PATH, url: str = DATA_URL) -> pd.DataFrame: if not path.exists(): logger.info(f"Downloading hotel bookings data to {path}") path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) raw = pd.read_csv(url) raw.to_csv(path, index=False) else: raw = pd.read_csv(path) df = raw[(raw["adr"] > 0) & (raw["adr"] <= 1000)].reset_index(drop=True) logger.info(f"Loaded {len(df):,} bookings") return df df_raw = load_hotel_data() # Features used in this notebook NUMERICAL_FEATURES: list[str] = [ "lead_time", "stays_in_weekend_nights", "stays_in_week_nights", "adults", "previous_cancellations", "booking_changes", "total_of_special_requests", # new: number of special requests made "days_in_waiting_list", # new: days the booking was on a waitlist ] CATEGORICAL_FEATURES: list[str] = [ "hotel", # City Hotel or Resort Hotel "meal", # Meal plan: BB, HB, FB, SC, Undefined "market_segment", # How the booking was made: Online TA, Direct, Corporate ... "distribution_channel",# Channel: TA/TO, Direct, Corporate, GDS "customer_type", # Transient, Contract, Transient-Party, Group "deposit_type", # No Deposit, Non Refund, Refundable ] ALL_FEATURES = NUMERICAL_FEATURES + CATEGORICAL_FEATURES TARGET_CLF = "is_canceled" df = df_raw[ALL_FEATURES + [TARGET_CLF]].copy() logger.info(f"Dataset: {df.shape[0]:,} rows, {len(NUMERICAL_FEATURES)} numerical, " f"{len(CATEGORICAL_FEATURES)} categorical features") ``` ::: ::: {#66394dc7 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Preview the categorical features: how many unique values does each have? print("Categorical feature value counts:") print(f" {'Feature':<25} {'Unique values':<20} {'Values'}") print(" " + "-" * 80) for col in CATEGORICAL_FEATURES: vals = sorted(df[col].unique().tolist()) print(f" {col:<25} {len(vals):<20} {vals}") print(f"\nTarget: {TARGET_CLF}") print(f" Cancellation rate: {df[TARGET_CLF].mean():.1%}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Categorical feature value counts: Feature Unique values Values -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- hotel 2 ['City Hotel', 'Resort Hotel'] meal 5 ['BB', 'FB', 'HB', 'SC', 'Undefined'] market_segment 8 ['Aviation', 'Complementary', 'Corporate', 'Direct', 'Groups', 'Offline TA/TO', 'Online TA', 'Undefined'] distribution_channel 5 ['Corporate', 'Direct', 'GDS', 'TA/TO', 'Undefined'] customer_type 4 ['Contract', 'Group', 'Transient', 'Transient-Party'] deposit_type 3 ['No Deposit', 'Non Refund', 'Refundable'] Target: is_canceled Cancellation rate: 37.5% ``` ::: ::: The categorical features have between 2 and 8 unique values each -- a good fit for **one-hot encoding**. Each category becomes a binary column (1 if the booking has that category, 0 otherwise). After one-hot encoding, the 6 categorical features expand into roughly 25-30 binary columns. The full feature matrix will have 8 (numerical) + ~28 (one-hot) = ~36 columns total. **Why not just use label encoding (0, 1, 2, ...)?** Label encoding implies an ordinal relationship: category 2 > category 1 > category 0. For `market_segment`, saying "Online TA > Direct > Corporate" is meaningless. One-hot encoding avoids this by treating all categories as equally different. ::: {#3e83cb99 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Split before building any pipeline or transformer. # This is the single most important sequencing rule in ML engineering. X_all = df[ALL_FEATURES] y_all = df[TARGET_CLF].to_numpy(dtype=int) X_train_all, X_test_all, y_train_clf, y_test_clf = train_test_split( X_all, y_all, test_size=0.20, random_state=42, stratify=y_all, ) logger.info(f"Train: {X_train_all.shape[0]:,} rows | Test: {X_test_all.shape[0]:,} rows") logger.info(f"Train cancellation rate: {y_train_clf.mean():.1%}") logger.info(f"Test cancellation rate: {y_test_clf.mean():.1%}") ``` ::: ## 1. The Pipeline Object In Part 25, preprocessing and modelling were two separate manual steps: ```python scaler = StandardScaler() X_train_s = scaler.fit_transform(X_train) # step 1 X_test_s = scaler.transform(X_test) model.fit(X_train_s, y_train) # step 2 model.predict(X_test_s) # step 3 ``` This works for a single train/test split. It **breaks inside cross-validation**, because you would need to carefully re-fit the scaler on each fold's training subset. In practice, most beginners fit the scaler once on all training data before calling `cross_val_score`, which leaks information across folds. **`Pipeline`** solves this by treating preprocessing + model as a single object. When `cross_val_score` calls `fit()` on a Pipeline, every step in the pipeline is re-fitted on that fold's training data. The scaler never sees the validation fold. Data leakage is structurally impossible. Building a Pipeline is straightforward. You pass a list of `(name, object)` tuples. The name is a string you choose; it is used later to set hyperparameters with the double-underscore syntax (`step_name__param_name`). ::: {#7dc4c517 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} # A simple Pipeline: StandardScaler then LogisticRegression # This replicates what Part 25 did manually, but as a single reusable object. pipe_simple = Pipeline([ ("scaler", StandardScaler()), ("model", LogisticRegression(class_weight="balanced", max_iter=500, random_state=42)), ]) # One call to fit() runs the full chain: scaler.fit_transform(X_train) then model.fit(...) pipe_simple.fit(X_train_all[NUMERICAL_FEATURES], y_train_clf) # One call to predict() runs: scaler.transform(X_test) then model.predict(...) y_pred_simple = pipe_simple.predict(X_test_all[NUMERICAL_FEATURES]) logger.info("Simple Pipeline (numerical features only):") logger.info(f" F1 (cancelled): {f1_score(y_test_clf, y_pred_simple):.4f}") # Inspect the pipeline structure print("Pipeline steps:") for name, step in pipe_simple.steps: print(f" [{name}] {type(step).__name__}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Pipeline steps: [scaler] StandardScaler [model] LogisticRegression ``` ::: :::
Key Concept: Pipeline = preprocessing + model as a single, leak-free object

A Pipeline is not just convenience. It is a correctness guarantee. When you pass a Pipeline to cross_val_score, sklearn internally calls pipeline.fit(X_fold_train, y_fold_train) for each fold. That triggers scaler.fit_transform(X_fold_train) -- the scaler is re-fitted on the fold's training data only. The validation rows are transformed with scaler.transform(X_fold_val) afterward. No fold ever contaminates another.
Activity 1: Inspect the fitted scaler inside the pipeline

After fitting pipe_simple, access the fitted scaler with pipe_simple.named_steps['scaler']. Print its mean_ and scale_ attributes. Do these match what you would get from fitting a standalone StandardScaler on X_train_all[NUMERICAL_FEATURES]?
::: {#dbdbc407 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Activity 1: your code here ``` ::: ## 2. ColumnTransformer: Mixed Feature Types The simple pipeline above uses only the 8 numerical features. The 6 categorical features are ignored, even though they contain strong signals (for example, bookings with a "Non Refund" deposit type almost never cancel, while bookings made through "Online TA" cancel at a much higher rate). To use both feature types, we need to apply: - **StandardScaler** to numerical columns (makes them zero-mean, unit-variance) - **OneHotEncoder** to categorical columns (converts each category to binary columns) `ColumnTransformer` does exactly this: it routes each subset of columns through its own transformer, then **concatenates the results** into a single feature matrix. ``` Input DataFrame (mixed columns) | |-- [NUMERICAL_FEATURES] --> StandardScaler --> scaled columns |-- [CATEGORICAL_FEATURES]--> OneHotEncoder --> binary columns | v Output: single numpy array (scaled + binary columns side by side) ``` ::: {#051934be .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Each transformer is (name, transformer_object, column_list) preprocessor = ColumnTransformer( transformers=[ ("num", StandardScaler(), NUMERICAL_FEATURES), ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False), CATEGORICAL_FEATURES), ] ) # handle_unknown="ignore": if a new category appears at inference time, # the encoder outputs all-zeros for that category instead of raising an error. # sparse_output=False: return a dense numpy array instead of a sparse matrix. # Fit on training data only, then inspect the output shape X_train_transformed = preprocessor.fit_transform(X_train_all) num_out_cols = len(NUMERICAL_FEATURES) cat_out_cols = X_train_transformed.shape[1] - num_out_cols logger.info("ColumnTransformer output:") logger.info(f" Input: {X_train_all.shape[1]} columns ({len(NUMERICAL_FEATURES)} numerical, " f"{len(CATEGORICAL_FEATURES)} categorical)") logger.info(f" Output: {X_train_transformed.shape[1]} columns " f"({num_out_cols} scaled numerical + {cat_out_cols} one-hot categorical)") # Show the feature names produced by the encoder cat_names = preprocessor.named_transformers_["cat"].get_feature_names_out(CATEGORICAL_FEATURES) print(f"\nCategorical features after one-hot encoding ({len(cat_names)} columns):") print(cat_names.tolist()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Categorical features after one-hot encoding (27 columns): ['hotel_City Hotel', 'hotel_Resort Hotel', 'meal_BB', 'meal_FB', 'meal_HB', 'meal_SC', 'meal_Undefined', 'market_segment_Aviation', 'market_segment_Complementary', 'market_segment_Corporate', 'market_segment_Direct', 'market_segment_Groups', 'market_segment_Offline TA/TO', 'market_segment_Online TA', 'market_segment_Undefined', 'distribution_channel_Corporate', 'distribution_channel_Direct', 'distribution_channel_GDS', 'distribution_channel_TA/TO', 'distribution_channel_Undefined', 'customer_type_Contract', 'customer_type_Group', 'customer_type_Transient', 'customer_type_Transient-Party', 'deposit_type_No Deposit', 'deposit_type_Non Refund', 'deposit_type_Refundable'] ``` ::: ::: **What just happened?** The 6 categorical columns were expanded into binary columns -- one per unique category value. For example, `meal` becomes `meal_BB`, `meal_FB`, `meal_HB`, `meal_SC`, `meal_Undefined`. Each booking gets a 1 in exactly one of those columns and 0 in the rest. The total output width (~36 columns) is what `LogisticRegression` actually sees. Each coefficient in the trained model corresponds to one of these columns. ::: {#c57ee6dc .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Now build the full Pipeline: ColumnTransformer + LogisticRegression pipe_full = Pipeline([ ("preprocessor", preprocessor), ("model", LogisticRegression(class_weight="balanced", max_iter=1000, random_state=42)), ]) pipe_full.fit(X_train_all, y_train_clf) y_pred_full = pipe_full.predict(X_test_all) logger.info("Full Pipeline (numerical + categorical features):") logger.info(f" F1 (cancelled): {f1_score(y_test_clf, y_pred_full):.4f}") print("Classification report (full feature set):") print(classification_report( y_test_clf, y_pred_full, target_names=["kept (0)", "cancelled (1)"] )) # Metrics comparison: numerical-only vs full feature set pipeline_comparison = pd.DataFrame([ {"pipeline": "Numerical only (6 features)", "f1": round(f1_score(y_test_clf, pipe_simple.predict(X_test_all[NUMERICAL_FEATURES])), 4)}, {"pipeline": "Numerical + Categorical (14 features)", "f1": round(f1_score(y_test_clf, y_pred_full), 4)}, ]) metrics_report( pipeline_comparison, metrics=["f1"], maximize_cols=["f1"], title="Pipeline Comparison: Feature Set Impact", subtitle="Adding categorical features improves cancellation detection", source_note="Hotel Booking Demand dataset (Antonio et al., 2019)", ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Classification report (full feature set): precision recall f1-score support kept (0) 0.82 0.82 0.82 14684 cancelled (1) 0.70 0.70 0.70 8802 accuracy 0.78 23486 macro avg 0.76 0.76 0.76 23486 weighted avg 0.78 0.78 0.78 23486 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=31} ```{=html}
Pipeline Comparison: Feature Set Impact
Adding categorical features improves cancellation detection
pipeline f1
Numerical only (6 features) 0.629
Numerical + Categorical (14 features) 0.703
Hotel Booking Demand dataset (Antonio et al., 2019)
``` ::: :::
Key Concept: ColumnTransformer routes columns, Pipeline chains steps

These two objects compose naturally. ColumnTransformer is used as the first step in a Pipeline. The pipeline re-fits the entire ColumnTransformer (including the OneHotEncoder) on each fold's training data during cross-validation. If a new category appears in a validation fold that was not in the training fold, handle_unknown='ignore' produces all-zeros for that column rather than crashing.
Activity 2: Compare numerical-only vs full feature set

Use cross_val_score with StratifiedKFold(5) and scoring='f1' to compare:
1. pipe_simple (numerical features only)
2. pipe_full (numerical + categorical features)
How much does adding categorical features improve the mean F1 score?
::: {#0c3c8c0a .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Activity 2: your code here ``` ::: ## 3. Why Pipeline Prevents Leakage The leakage problem from Part 25 Section 3 reappears whenever preprocessing is done outside the Pipeline before calling `cross_val_score`. Here we demonstrate the exact numerical difference between the two approaches. **The wrong approach:** fit the scaler and encoder on the **entire** training set, then pass the already-transformed array to `cross_val_score`. Every fold uses transformers that were influenced by validation-fold rows during fitting. ::: {#7936d80d .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} # WRONG: Fit transformers on all training data before cross-validation # (this is done deliberately to show the problem) preprocessor_leaky = ColumnTransformer( transformers=[ ("num", StandardScaler(), NUMERICAL_FEATURES), ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False), CATEGORICAL_FEATURES), ] ) # fit_transform on ALL training data -- this is the mistake X_train_leaky = preprocessor_leaky.fit_transform(X_train_all) from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold cv_leaky = cross_val_score( LogisticRegression(class_weight="balanced", max_iter=1000, random_state=42), X_train_leaky, # already transformed -- leaks fold info y_train_clf, cv=StratifiedKFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=42), scoring="f1", ) # CORRECT: pass the Pipeline (with preprocessing inside) to cross_val_score cv_correct = cross_val_score( pipe_full, # Pipeline re-fits transformers per fold X_train_all, # raw DataFrame -- no pre-transformation y_train_clf, cv=StratifiedKFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=42), scoring="f1", ) logger.info(f"WRONG (leaky preprocessing): F1 = {cv_leaky.mean():.4f} +/- {cv_leaky.std():.4f}") logger.info(f"CORRECT (Pipeline inside CV): F1 = {cv_correct.mean():.4f} +/- {cv_correct.std():.4f}") logger.info( f"Score inflation from leakage: +{cv_leaky.mean() - cv_correct.mean():.4f} F1 points" ) ``` :::
Common Mistake: Fitting preprocessing outside the Pipeline before cross_val_score

The inflation from leakage is typically small on large, balanced datasets but grows as:
(1) The dataset shrinks (each fold represents a larger fraction of training data).
(2) The transformation is more data-dependent (e.g., PCA, target encoding, feature selection based on correlation).
(3) The folds become more heterogeneous (temporal or group-based splits).

The correct pattern is always: raw data in, Pipeline out of cross-validation. Never pre-transform before calling any cross-validation function.
## 4. Hyperparameter Search: GridSearchCV and RandomizedSearchCV Every sklearn estimator has hyperparameters: settings that are fixed before training and control how the model learns. For `LogisticRegression`, the key one is: - **`C`** (regularisation strength): smaller `C` = stronger regularisation = simpler model. Default is 1.0. Good values often span several orders of magnitude: 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100. Inside a Pipeline, hyperparameter names follow the **double-underscore naming convention**: `step_name__param_name`. To set `LogisticRegression`'s `C`, you write `model__C`. To set `StandardScaler`'s `with_mean` flag, you write `preprocessor__num__with_mean`. **`GridSearchCV`** exhaustively evaluates every combination in a grid. If you have 5 values for `C` and 3 values for `max_iter`, GridSearchCV trains 5 x 3 = 15 models (times 5 CV folds = 75 fits). This is manageable but grows exponentially with more hyperparameters. ::: {#865aa0be .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} # GridSearchCV: define the grid as a dict # Keys follow the double-underscore naming convention: step__param param_grid = { "model__C": [0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 100.0], "model__max_iter": [500, 1000], } grid_search = GridSearchCV( pipe_full, param_grid, cv=StratifiedKFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=42), scoring="f1", n_jobs=-1, verbose=0, refit=True, # after finding best params, refit on ALL training data ) grid_search.fit(X_train_all, y_train_clf) logger.info(f"GridSearchCV -- best params: {grid_search.best_params_}") logger.info(f"Best CV F1: {grid_search.best_score_:.4f}") # Evaluate on the held-out test set y_pred_grid = grid_search.predict(X_test_all) logger.info(f"Test F1 (best GridSearch model): {f1_score(y_test_clf, y_pred_grid):.4f}") ``` ::: **`RandomizedSearchCV`** samples a fixed number of combinations at random from distributions (or lists). It is better than GridSearch when: - The search space has more than ~3 hyperparameters. - You want to search continuous ranges (e.g., `C` on a log scale from 0.001 to 100). - Training is slow and you cannot afford to evaluate every combination. With `n_iter=30` and the same 5-fold CV, RandomizedSearchCV trains 150 models -- the same as GridSearch with a 5x6 grid -- but explores the space more efficiently because it can sample from a continuous distribution. ::: {#6e3c65f5 .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} # RandomizedSearchCV: sample from distributions instead of a fixed grid param_dist = { "model__C": loguniform(0.001, 100), # sample log-uniformly from [0.001, 100] "model__max_iter": [500, 1000, 2000], } rand_search = RandomizedSearchCV( pipe_full, param_dist, n_iter=30, cv=StratifiedKFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=42), scoring="f1", random_state=42, n_jobs=-1, refit=True, ) rand_search.fit(X_train_all, y_train_clf) logger.info(f"RandomizedSearchCV -- best params: {rand_search.best_params_}") logger.info(f"Best CV F1: {rand_search.best_score_:.4f}") y_pred_rand = rand_search.predict(X_test_all) logger.info(f"Test F1 (best RandomSearch model): {f1_score(y_test_clf, y_pred_rand):.4f}") # Summary table comparing search strategies search_summary = pd.DataFrame([ {"strategy": "Default (C=1.0)", "best_C": 1.0, "cv_f1": round(cross_val_score(pipe_full, X_train_all, y_train_clf, cv=StratifiedKFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=42), scoring="f1", n_jobs=-1).mean(), 4), "test_f1": round(f1_score(y_test_clf, pipe_full.predict(X_test_all)), 4)}, {"strategy": "GridSearchCV", "best_C": round(grid_search.best_params_["model__C"], 4), "cv_f1": round(grid_search.best_score_, 4), "test_f1": round(f1_score(y_test_clf, y_pred_grid), 4)}, {"strategy": "RandomizedSearchCV", "best_C": round(rand_search.best_params_["model__C"], 4), "cv_f1": round(rand_search.best_score_, 4), "test_f1": round(f1_score(y_test_clf, y_pred_rand), 4)}, ]) metrics_report( search_summary, metrics=["best_C", "cv_f1", "test_f1"], maximize_cols=["cv_f1", "test_f1"], title="Hyperparameter Search Comparison", subtitle="CV F1 = mean 5-fold F1 on training set | Test F1 = held-out set", source_note="Hotel Booking Demand dataset (Antonio et al., 2019)", ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=35} ```{=html}
Hyperparameter Search Comparison
CV F1 = mean 5-fold F1 on training set | Test F1 = held-out set
strategy best_C cv_f1 test_f1
Default (C=1.0) 1.000 0.699 0.703
GridSearchCV 100.000 0.699 0.702
RandomizedSearchCV 0.075 0.699 0.702
Hotel Booking Demand dataset (Antonio et al., 2019)
``` ::: :::
Key Concept: Double-underscore naming lets you tune any step in a Pipeline

The convention is step_name__param_name. For a nested step (Pipeline inside ColumnTransformer), chain with more underscores: preprocessor__num__with_mean reaches the StandardScaler's with_mean parameter.

You can explore all available parameter names with pipe_full.get_params() -- this returns a flat dict of every tunable parameter in the pipeline.
Activity 3: Visualise the search results

From grid_search.cv_results_, extract the mean test F1 for each combination of C and max_iter. Plot a heatmap (or grouped bar chart) showing F1 vs C. At what value of C does the model peak? Does max_iter matter?
::: {#2473bbca .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Activity 3: your code here ``` ::: ## 5. Optuna: Production-Grade Hyperparameter Search GridSearchCV and RandomizedSearchCV evaluate each trial independently. Optuna improves on this by using a **Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE)** -- a Bayesian-style algorithm that models the relationship between hyperparameters and objective value, and samples the next trial from regions that look promising based on previous results. **Practical advantages over GridSearch/RandomSearch:** | Feature | GridSearch | RandomSearch | Optuna | |---|---|---|---| | Learns from past trials | No | No | Yes (TPE) | | Continuous search spaces | No | Yes | Yes | | Prune bad trials early | No | No | Yes | | Parallel execution | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Handles complex dependencies | No | No | Yes | Optuna is already in the project's `[modelling]` extra dependencies (see `pyproject.toml`). Install with `uv sync --extra modelling`. ::: {#12b36cc0 .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} # An Optuna objective function takes a Trial and returns the value to maximise/minimise def objective(trial: optuna.Trial) -> float: # Suggest hyperparameter values -- Optuna learns which regions to explore C = trial.suggest_float("C", 0.001, 100.0, log=True) # log scale: samples 0.001, 0.01, 0.1 ... max_iter = trial.suggest_int("max_iter", 200, 2000, step=200) # Build a fresh pipeline for this trial pipe_trial = Pipeline([ ("preprocessor", ColumnTransformer( transformers=[ ("num", StandardScaler(), NUMERICAL_FEATURES), ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False), CATEGORICAL_FEATURES), ] )), ("model", LogisticRegression( C=C, max_iter=max_iter, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42, )), ]) # Return mean CV F1 -- Optuna will maximise this return cross_val_score( pipe_trial, X_train_all, y_train_clf, cv=StratifiedKFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=42), scoring="f1", n_jobs=-1, ).mean() # Create a study (direction="maximize" because F1 is better when higher) study = optuna.create_study(direction="maximize") study.optimize(objective, n_trials=40, show_progress_bar=False) logger.info(f"Optuna best trial: {study.best_params}") logger.info(f"Best CV F1: {study.best_value:.4f}") ``` ::: ::: {#98bdc532 .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Build and evaluate the best pipeline found by Optuna best_C = study.best_params["C"] best_max_iter = study.best_params["max_iter"] best_pipe = Pipeline([ ("preprocessor", ColumnTransformer( transformers=[ ("num", StandardScaler(), NUMERICAL_FEATURES), ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False), CATEGORICAL_FEATURES), ] )), ("model", LogisticRegression( C=best_C, max_iter=best_max_iter, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42, )), ]) best_pipe.fit(X_train_all, y_train_clf) y_pred_optuna = best_pipe.predict(X_test_all) print("Optuna best pipeline -- test set performance:") print(classification_report( y_test_clf, y_pred_optuna, target_names=["kept (0)", "cancelled (1)"] )) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Optuna best pipeline -- test set performance: precision recall f1-score support kept (0) 0.82 0.82 0.82 14684 cancelled (1) 0.70 0.70 0.70 8802 accuracy 0.78 23486 macro avg 0.76 0.76 0.76 23486 weighted avg 0.78 0.78 0.78 23486 ``` ::: ::: **Visualise the search:** The optimisation history shows how Optuna narrowed in on good hyperparameter regions over 40 trials. Early trials explore broadly; later trials concentrate near the best observed region. ::: {#a5211883 .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Optimisation history: how did the best score improve over trials? trial_df = study.trials_dataframe()[["number", "value", "params_C", "params_max_iter"]] trial_df["best_so_far"] = trial_df["value"].cummax() ( ggplot(trial_df, aes(x="number", y="value")) + geom_point(alpha=0.5, size=2, color=INFO) + geom_line(aes(y="best_so_far"), color=DANGER, size=1.2) + labs( title="Optuna Optimisation History (40 trials)", subtitle="Blue dots: each trial F1. Red line: best F1 found so far.", x="Trial Number", y="CV F1 Score", ) + modern_theme(grid=True) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=39} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
Pro Tip: Use Optuna when GridSearch becomes too slow

GridSearch with 3 hyperparameters each taking 5 values means 5^3 = 125 combinations times 5 folds = 625 fits. Adding a 4th hyperparameter makes it 3,125 fits. Optuna typically achieves the same result in 50-100 trials because it focuses on promising regions. As a rule of thumb: use GridSearch for 1-2 hyperparameters with a small grid, RandomSearch for 3-4 with medium budgets, and Optuna for anything larger or when you want to tune multiple hyperparameters simultaneously.
Activity 4: Add a third hyperparameter to the Optuna study

Extend the objective function to also tune solver from the set ['lbfgs', 'saga', 'liblinear'] (use trial.suggest_categorical). Run 50 trials and check: does adding the solver choice improve the best F1?
::: {#266d9e6e .cell execution_count=17} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Activity 4: your code here ``` ::: ## 6. Feature Importance Once the model is trained, two practical questions arise: 1. **Sanity check**: are the most important features the ones that make domain sense? If `lead_time` is the top predictor of cancellation, that is reassuring (bookings made far in advance cancel more often). If `adults` is the top predictor, that is suspicious. 2. **Debugging**: if a feature that should matter shows near-zero importance, something may be wrong with the data pipeline or the feature encoding. For `LogisticRegression`, the learned coefficients (accessible via `model.coef_`) are the feature weights. Because the numerical features were standardised, their coefficients are directly comparable in magnitude. ::: {#2188eba3 .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} def get_feature_names(pipe: Pipeline) -> list[str]: """Return the full list of feature names after ColumnTransformer.""" ct: ColumnTransformer = pipe.named_steps["preprocessor"] num_names = NUMERICAL_FEATURES cat_names = ( ct.named_transformers_["cat"] .get_feature_names_out(CATEGORICAL_FEATURES) .tolist() ) return num_names + cat_names feature_names = get_feature_names(best_pipe) coefs = best_pipe.named_steps["model"].coef_[0] importance_df = ( pd.DataFrame({"feature": feature_names, "coefficient": coefs}) .assign(abs_coef=lambda x: x["coefficient"].abs()) .sort_values("abs_coef", ascending=False) .head(20) ) print("Top 20 features by absolute coefficient:") print(importance_df[["feature", "coefficient"]].to_string(index=False)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Top 20 features by absolute coefficient: feature coefficient deposit_type_Non Refund 3.328234 deposit_type_No Deposit -1.850028 previous_cancellations 1.490870 deposit_type_Refundable -1.260259 market_segment_Online TA 0.920878 meal_FB 0.803175 customer_type_Transient 0.605325 total_of_special_requests -0.558551 market_segment_Offline TA/TO -0.522381 customer_type_Group -0.502092 market_segment_Corporate -0.444245 lead_time 0.427787 meal_Undefined -0.392942 distribution_channel_TA/TO 0.266300 booking_changes -0.259249 customer_type_Transient-Party 0.231679 distribution_channel_GDS -0.229954 hotel_City Hotel 0.206473 distribution_channel_Corporate 0.174446 distribution_channel_Undefined 0.171832 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#df58559f .cell execution_count=19} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Plot the top features plot_df = importance_df.copy() plot_df["color"] = plot_df["coefficient"].apply( lambda x: "Increases cancellation risk" if x > 0 else "Decreases cancellation risk" ) ( ggplot(plot_df, aes(x="reorder(feature, abs_coef)", y="coefficient", fill="color")) + geom_bar(stat="identity") + scale_fill_manual( values={ "Increases cancellation risk": DANGER, "Decreases cancellation risk": INFO, } ) + coord_flip() + labs( title="Top 20 Feature Coefficients (LogisticRegression)", subtitle="Red bars increase cancellation probability; blue bars decrease it", x="", y="Coefficient", fill="", ) + modern_theme(legend_pos="bottom") ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=42} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: **Interpreting the coefficients:** - Positive coefficients increase the log-odds of cancellation; negative coefficients decrease it. - `deposit_type_Non Refund` should have a very large **negative** coefficient: bookings with a non-refundable deposit almost never cancel (the guest would lose their money). - `lead_time` should have a positive coefficient: the further ahead a booking is made, the more likely it is to cancel. - If these are not true in your output, there may be a data quality issue worth investigating. **Forward pointer:** For tree-based models, use `feature_importances_` instead of coefficients. For model-agnostic importance across all model types, SHAP values provide a unified framework -- covered in depth in the Part 5 projects.
Pro Tip: Always sanity-check feature importance against domain knowledge

A model that achieves good cross-validated F1 but has bizarre feature importances is a red flag. Possible causes: data leakage (a post-arrival feature that perfectly predicts whether the booking cancelled because it is derived from the outcome), label encoding instead of one-hot, or a feature that is highly correlated with the target by construction.
## 7. Model Card A model card is structured documentation for a trained model. It records what the model predicts, which data and features it uses, how it was evaluated, and its known limitations. This is the completed version of the problem spec card from Chapter 3. The model card serves two audiences: - **Future you** in 6 months, when you need to remember why you made certain choices. - **Stakeholders and reviewers** who need to understand what the model does before deploying it or approving it for use. ::: {#77288377 .cell execution_count=20} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Compute final CV metrics on the best pipeline cv_final = cross_val_score( best_pipe, X_train_all, y_train_clf, cv=StratifiedKFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=42), scoring="f1", n_jobs=-1, ) test_f1 = f1_score(y_test_clf, y_pred_optuna) model_card = { "model_name": "hotel_cancellation_classifier_v1", "task": "Binary classification", "prediction": "Will a hotel booking cancel before arrival? (1=yes, 0=no)", "dataset": { "name": "Hotel Booking Demand (Antonio et al., 2019)", "rows_train": int(X_train_all.shape[0]), "rows_test": int(X_test_all.shape[0]), "cancellation_rate": f"{y_train_clf.mean():.1%}", }, "features": { "numerical": NUMERICAL_FEATURES, "categorical": CATEGORICAL_FEATURES, "total_after_encoding": len(feature_names), }, "model": { "type": "LogisticRegression", "hyperparameters": study.best_params, "class_weight": "balanced", }, "evaluation": { "metric": "F1 (cancelled class)", "cv_mean": round(float(cv_final.mean()), 4), "cv_std": round(float(cv_final.std()), 4), "test_f1": round(test_f1, 4), }, "known_limitations": [ "Trained on 2015-2017 data from two Portuguese hotels; performance may " "degrade on newer data or different markets.", "LogisticRegression captures only linear decision boundaries; " "non-linear interactions between features are not modelled.", "Cancellation patterns may differ significantly across seasons; " "a time-stratified evaluation would be more robust.", ], } import pprint pprint.pprint(model_card, width=80) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` {'dataset': {'cancellation_rate': '37.5%', 'name': 'Hotel Booking Demand (Antonio et al., 2019)', 'rows_test': 23486, 'rows_train': 93943}, 'evaluation': {'cv_mean': 0.699, 'cv_std': 0.0035, 'metric': 'F1 (cancelled class)', 'test_f1': 0.7018}, 'features': {'categorical': ['hotel', 'meal', 'market_segment', 'distribution_channel', 'customer_type', 'deposit_type'], 'numerical': ['lead_time', 'stays_in_weekend_nights', 'stays_in_week_nights', 'adults', 'previous_cancellations', 'booking_changes', 'total_of_special_requests', 'days_in_waiting_list'], 'total_after_encoding': 35}, 'known_limitations': ['Trained on 2015-2017 data from two Portuguese hotels; ' 'performance may degrade on newer data or different ' 'markets.', 'LogisticRegression captures only linear decision ' 'boundaries; non-linear interactions between features ' 'are not modelled.', 'Cancellation patterns may differ significantly across ' 'seasons; a time-stratified evaluation would be more ' 'robust.'], 'model': {'class_weight': 'balanced', 'hyperparameters': {'C': 0.12241490042618425, 'max_iter': 1000}, 'type': 'LogisticRegression'}, 'model_name': 'hotel_cancellation_classifier_v1', 'prediction': 'Will a hotel booking cancel before arrival? (1=yes, 0=no)', 'task': 'Binary classification'} ``` ::: ::: ::: {#2bc665e7 .cell execution_count=21} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Save the model card alongside the model artefact MODEL_DIR = Path("models") MODEL_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True) artefact = { "pipeline": best_pipe, "features": ALL_FEATURES, "target": TARGET_CLF, "model_card": model_card, } artefact_path = MODEL_DIR / "hotel_cancellation_pipeline_v1.joblib" joblib.dump(artefact, artefact_path) logger.info(f"Full artefact saved to {artefact_path} " f"({artefact_path.stat().st_size / 1024:.1f} KB)") ``` ::: ## Capstone: End-to-End Pipeline Function Combine everything into a single reusable function that accepts a DataFrame, feature lists, and a target, then runs the complete production workflow: 1. Train/test split with stratification. 2. Build a `Pipeline` with `ColumnTransformer`. 3. Run an Optuna study (`n_trials` configurable). 4. Evaluate the best pipeline on the held-out test set. 5. Save the artefact (pipeline + model card) with `joblib`. 6. Return a model card dict. This function follows the same structure as the Part 5 project notebooks, which apply it to different business datasets. ::: {#689d5e61 .cell execution_count=22} ``` {.python .cell-code} def build_classification_pipeline( df: pd.DataFrame, numerical_features: list[str], categorical_features: list[str], target: str, n_trials: int = 40, test_size: float = 0.2, random_state: int = 42, model_dir: Path = Path("models"), ) -> dict[str, Any]: """Full pipeline: split, ColumnTransformer, Optuna search, evaluate, save. Returns: A model card dict with dataset stats, best hyperparameters, and metrics. """ all_features = numerical_features + categorical_features X = df[all_features] y = df[target].to_numpy(dtype=int) X_tr, X_te, y_tr, y_te = train_test_split( X, y, test_size=test_size, random_state=random_state, stratify=y ) logger.info(f"Split: {X_tr.shape[0]:,} train / {X_te.shape[0]:,} test") def _objective(trial: optuna.Trial) -> float: C = trial.suggest_float("C", 0.001, 100.0, log=True) max_iter = trial.suggest_int("max_iter", 200, 2000, step=200) pipe = Pipeline([ ("preprocessor", ColumnTransformer( transformers=[ ("num", StandardScaler(), numerical_features), ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False), categorical_features), ] )), ("model", LogisticRegression( C=C, max_iter=max_iter, class_weight="balanced", random_state=random_state, )), ]) return cross_val_score( pipe, X_tr, y_tr, cv=StratifiedKFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=random_state), scoring="f1", n_jobs=-1, ).mean() study_fn = optuna.create_study(direction="maximize") study_fn.optimize(_objective, n_trials=n_trials, show_progress_bar=False) logger.info(f"Optuna finished: best_params={study_fn.best_params}, " f"best_cv_f1={study_fn.best_value:.4f}") best = study_fn.best_params final_pipe = Pipeline([ ("preprocessor", ColumnTransformer( transformers=[ ("num", StandardScaler(), numerical_features), ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False), categorical_features), ] )), ("model", LogisticRegression( C=best["C"], max_iter=best["max_iter"], class_weight="balanced", random_state=random_state, )), ]) final_pipe.fit(X_tr, y_tr) test_f1 = round(f1_score(y_te, final_pipe.predict(X_te)), 4) logger.info(f"Test F1: {test_f1}") card: dict[str, Any] = { "model_type": "LogisticRegression", "target": target, "numerical_features": numerical_features, "categorical_features": categorical_features, "best_hyperparameters": best, "cv_f1": round(study_fn.best_value, 4), "test_f1": test_f1, "train_rows": int(X_tr.shape[0]), "test_rows": int(X_te.shape[0]), } model_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) save_path = model_dir / f"pipeline_{target}.joblib" joblib.dump({"pipeline": final_pipe, "card": card}, save_path) logger.info(f"Saved to {save_path}") return card ``` ::: ::: {#d1664432 .cell execution_count=23} ``` {.python .cell-code} import shutil MODEL_OUT = Path("models") if MODEL_OUT.exists(): shutil.rmtree(MODEL_OUT) # clean slate for the capstone run card = build_classification_pipeline( df=df, numerical_features=NUMERICAL_FEATURES, categorical_features=CATEGORICAL_FEATURES, target=TARGET_CLF, n_trials=40, model_dir=MODEL_OUT, ) import pprint print("\nCapstone model card:") pprint.pprint(card, width=80) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Capstone model card: {'best_hyperparameters': {'C': 4.544875463250714, 'max_iter': 1800}, 'categorical_features': ['hotel', 'meal', 'market_segment', 'distribution_channel', 'customer_type', 'deposit_type'], 'cv_f1': 0.699, 'model_type': 'LogisticRegression', 'numerical_features': ['lead_time', 'stays_in_weekend_nights', 'stays_in_week_nights', 'adults', 'previous_cancellations', 'booking_changes', 'total_of_special_requests', 'days_in_waiting_list'], 'target': 'is_canceled', 'test_f1': 0.7023, 'test_rows': 23486, 'train_rows': 93943} ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" icon=false} ## Further Reading | Resource | What to read | |---|---| | [sklearn User Guide: Pipeline](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/compose.html) | Nested pipelines, FeatureUnion, set_output API | | [sklearn User Guide: ColumnTransformer](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/compose.html#columntransformer-for-heterogeneous-data) | remainder="passthrough", FunctionTransformer, custom transformers | | [sklearn User Guide: GridSearchCV](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/grid_search.html) | HalvingGridSearch, HalvingRandomSearch for faster search | | [Optuna documentation](https://optuna.readthedocs.io/) | Pruners, samplers, multi-objective optimisation, distributed search | | [SHAP documentation](https://shap.readthedocs.io/) | Model-agnostic feature importance for any sklearn-compatible model | | Geron, A. (2022). *Hands-On Machine Learning*, 3rd ed. (Ch. 2, 4) | GridSearchCV and Pipeline in a full real-world project | ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" icon=false} ## Summary | Concept | Key takeaway | |---|---| | **Pipeline** | Chains preprocessing + model; `fit()` re-runs all steps; prevents leakage structurally | | **ColumnTransformer** | Routes feature subsets through different transformers and concatenates results | | **OneHotEncoder** | Converts categories to binary columns; `handle_unknown="ignore"` for robustness | | **Double-underscore naming** | `step_name__param` to set any pipeline parameter in GridSearchCV | | **GridSearchCV** | Exhaustive search; correct for small grids (under ~100 combinations) | | **RandomizedSearchCV** | Samples from distributions; better for larger or continuous search spaces | | **Optuna** | Learns from past trials (TPE); use for 3+ hyperparameters or slow training | | **Feature importance** | `coef_` for linear models; sanity-check against domain knowledge | | **Model card** | Documents model intent, features, hyperparameters, metrics, and known limitations | ::: **Part 4 complete.** You have built the full conceptual and practical foundation: the AI landscape (Part 22), ML taxonomy (Part 23), the workflow (Part 24), the sklearn API (Part 25), and production-grade pipelines (Part 26). **Next:** Part 5 applies everything to five end-to-end business projects using real datasets -- bike demand forecasting, customer churn, energy anomaly detection, customer segmentation, and movie recommendations.
